/blog/whats-next.title: What's Next - Majd
/blog/whats-next.description: Computer Science student and software developer passionate about building modern web experiences, Android applications, AI-powered tools, and intuitive user interfaces.
h2: A Journey Without a Finish Line
p: Rudraksh

Every completed project marks the end of one chapter and the beginning of another. This article reflects on the path ahead, the technologies I want to explore, and the mindset I hope to carry throughout my journey as a developer.
5 min read
"The future belongs to those who keep building."

Looking back at the projects I've completed, I don't see finished products—I see milestones. Every application, experiment, and late-night debugging session represents another step toward becoming the developer I aspire to be.
Technology never stands still, and neither should the people building it. That constant evolution is what makes software development both challenging and exciting.
For me, every finished project is simply the beginning of the next idea.
Each project has taught me something different, but they all share one thing in common—they've encouraged me to think beyond my comfort zone.
As I continue learning, I want to take on more ambitious challenges by building applications that combine thoughtful design, scalable architecture, and intelligent technologies.
I'm particularly interested in exploring:
Artificial Intelligence and automation
Modern full-stack web development
Android application development
Cloud technologies and deployment
User experience and product design
These areas represent not only the future of technology but also the direction I want my work to take.
One lesson has become increasingly clear throughout my journey:
Curiosity is one of the most valuable skills a developer can have.
Programming languages will change.
Frameworks will evolve.
Tools will be replaced.
But the willingness to learn, adapt, and solve problems will always remain valuable.
That's the mindset I hope to carry into every future project.
I don't want to build software simply because I can.
I want to build products that solve meaningful problems, improve everyday experiences, and leave a positive impact on the people who use them.
Whether it's a productivity tool, an AI-powered application, or a simple website, every project should exist for a reason beyond adding another repository to GitHub.
Purpose will always matter more than complexity.
One of the things I love most about technology is that there is always something new to discover.
Every new framework introduces fresh ideas.
Every project teaches unexpected lessons.
Every challenge becomes another opportunity to improve.
That endless cycle of learning is what makes this journey so rewarding.
If you've explored this portfolio, read these journal entries, or taken the time to understand my work, thank you.
This portfolio isn't just a showcase of projects—it's a snapshot of where I am today.
Years from now, I hope to look back at these pages and see not only how much my skills have grown, but also how my curiosity, creativity, and passion for building have remained exactly the same.
The best projects are still ahead.

/blog/design-before-development.title: Design Before Development - Majd
/blog/design-before-development.description: Computer Science student and software developer passionate about building modern web experiences, Android applications, AI-powered tools, and intuitive user interfaces.
p: Rudraksh

While development brings ideas to life, design determines how people experience them. This article explores why I believe planning, user experience, and visual clarity should come before writing the first line of code.
6 min read

Writing code is one of the most exciting parts of building software, but I've learned that successful products rarely begin there. Every meaningful application starts with understanding the people who will use it and designing an experience around their needs.

Before creating interfaces or implementing features, I spend time thinking about structure, navigation, and how every interaction should feel. A well-planned design reduces complexity, improves usability, and makes development far more efficient.
Good design isn't about making something look impressive. It's about helping users achieve their goals without confusion or unnecessary effort.
When designing an interface, I focus on questions like:
Is the navigation intuitive?
Can users complete tasks without guidance?
Does the layout feel balanced?
Is the interface accessible across different devices?
Answering these questions early creates products that feel natural instead of overwhelming.
Minimal interfaces often appear effortless, but achieving simplicity requires careful decision-making.

Typography, spacing, hierarchy, and color all influence how people interact with a product. Removing unnecessary elements doesn't reduce value—it strengthens it by allowing users to focus on what truly matters.
I've learned that the best interfaces rarely demand attention. They quietly guide users toward their goals through clarity and consistency.
Every project reminds me that software is ultimately built for people, not developers.
Users don't think about frameworks, programming languages, or architectural decisions. They care about whether an application feels reliable, understandable, and enjoyable to use.
Keeping that perspective helps me prioritize meaningful experiences over unnecessary features.
Design and development should never compete—they should complement one another.

A thoughtful design provides direction, while clean development transforms ideas into reliable products. Throughout my projects, I've found that balancing both disciplines creates software that is easier to maintain, easier to expand, and more satisfying to use.
Every improvement in design ultimately makes development stronger.
As I continue building projects, I want to deepen my understanding of user experience, interaction design, and accessibility alongside software development.
Technology will continue evolving, but creating experiences that genuinely help people will always remain the foundation of great digital products.
Design is more than appearance—it's the process of making technology feel approachable.

Every project I build reinforces the importance of thoughtful planning, clear communication, and intentional decision-making. By designing before developing, I aim to create products that are not only functional but memorable, intuitive, and built with purpose.

/blog/designing-my-portfolio.title: Designing My Portfolio - Majd
/blog/designing-my-portfolio.description: Computer Science student and software developer passionate about building modern web experiences, Android applications, AI-powered tools, and intuitive user interfaces.
p: Rudraksh

A portfolio is more than a collection of projects—it's a reflection of who you are as a developer. This article explores the ideas, design decisions, and challenges behind creating a space that feels authentic, minimal, and purpose-driven.
6 min read

A portfolio should do more than display projects—it should communicate personality, process, and purpose. When I started designing mine, I didn't want another template filled with generic layouts and flashy animations. I wanted something that felt clean, intentional, and uniquely mine.

Every decision, from typography and spacing to interactions and structure, was guided by one goal: creating an experience that allows visitors to focus on the work rather than unnecessary distractions.

The first challenge wasn't writing code—it was deciding how I wanted my work to be presented. I explored different styles, experimented with layouts, and analyzed portfolios from designers and developers whose work inspired me.
Instead of following trends, I focused on creating an editorial experience with generous whitespace, bold typography, and subtle interactions that encourage visitors to explore naturally.

Minimal design often appears effortless, but achieving simplicity requires countless refinements. Small adjustments to spacing, alignment, typography, and hierarchy can completely change how a page feels.
Throughout the design process, I continuously removed unnecessary elements until every section had a clear purpose and every interaction felt intentional.
As both the designer and developer, I needed to think from two perspectives. The interface had to look visually refined while remaining responsive, accessible, and technically maintainable.
Building the portfolio became an opportunity to combine creativity with engineering—where every design decision needed to translate into a functional, scalable implementation.
This portfolio isn't a finished product. Like every project I build, it continues to evolve as I learn new technologies, improve my design thinking, and complete more ambitious work.
Each update represents another step in my journey, making the portfolio not just a showcase of projects, but a record of continuous growth.

My goal is to keep expanding this portfolio with new case studies, development notes, and personal reflections. As I build more applications and explore emerging technologies, this space will continue to document both my work and the lessons learned along the way.
Rather than being a final destination, this portfolio is the beginning of a much longer journey—one built through curiosity, creativity, and continuous learning.

Every project featured here represents an idea transformed into reality. Together, they tell the story of my growth as a developer, designer, and problem solver, while offering a glimpse into the work I hope to build in the future.

/blog/from-student-to-developer.title: From Student to Developer - Majd
/blog/from-student-to-developer.description: Computer Science student and software developer passionate about building modern web experiences, Android applications, AI-powered tools, and intuitive user interfaces.
h2: What does purposeful product design mean?
The importance of solving real user problems
p: Rudraksh

Growth isn't measured by certificates or completed courses—it's measured by the problems you solve, the projects you build, and the lessons you carry forward. This article reflects on my journey as a student learning through curiosity, experimentation, and continuous improvement.
6 min read
Purposeful digital product design starts with a simple idea:every interface and interaction should solve a real problem.
“Good design isn’t about adding more. It’s about understanding what truly matters.”

Instead of chasing visual trends, purposeful design is grounded in empathy, clarity, and thoughtful iteration. It’s about creating experiences that genuinely support the user, not just impress them visually.

A product becomes meaningful when its core functionality directly aligns with users’ needs. This is where research plays a critical role — talking to users, observing their behavior, and continuously testing assumptions.
A few guiding principles:
Understand the problem before designing the solution
Let function lead form
Simplify wherever possible
When teams follow this mindset, interfaces feel intuitive even on the first visit.
Many platforms try to offer everything at once, assuming more features equal more value. In reality, this usually leads to clutter, confusion, and a fragmented experience.
Purposeful design takes the opposite approach:
Remove what isn’t needed
Highlight the elements that matter most
Guide users toward a clear, focused goal
Less noise, more clarity.

Visual design should support the experience, not compete with it. Clean spacing, readable typography, and consistent colors help users absorb information quickly. Motion and micro-interactions can add delight, but they should remain subtle and intentional.

Truly impactful products are created when everyone works toward the same vision. Designers, developers, product managers, and marketing teams each shape different layers of the final experience. Purposeful collaboration leads to purposeful outcomes.
If you’re looking to create a beautiful, intentional portfolio without starting from scratch, explore our free Framer templates.
They offer:
Clean, modern layouts
Smooth interactions
A professional look ready to personalize
A fast, polished foundation for any portfolio.

/blog/inside-securevault.title: Inside SecureVault - Majd
/blog/inside-securevault.description: Computer Science student and software developer passionate about building modern web experiences, Android applications, AI-powered tools, and intuitive user interfaces.
p: Rudraksh

SecureVault began as a simple idea to protect files securely, but quickly evolved into one of my most valuable learning experiences. From designing the interface to implementing encryption and authentication, every stage pushed me to think beyond writing code and focus on building a reliable product.
8 min read

Every developer has a project that changes the way they think about software development. For me, that project was SecureVault. What started as an experiment gradually evolved into an application that challenged me to learn about security, interface design, application architecture, and user experience all at the same time.

Rather than creating another simple file manager, I wanted to build something that users could trust. That meant thinking beyond functionality and considering how every feature contributed to privacy, reliability, and ease of use.

The initial goal was straightforward: create a secure place where users could protect their important files. While the concept sounded simple, implementing it required much more planning than expected.
I had to consider questions such as:
How should users authenticate securely?
How can sensitive data remain protected?
How should files be organized?
What interface would feel simple without sacrificing functionality?
These early decisions shaped the foundation of the project.

As development progressed, SecureVault became much more than its original concept. Every new feature introduced additional challenges that required research, experimentation, and continuous refinement.

Instead of focusing only on adding functionality, I spent time improving the overall experience by making navigation intuitive, simplifying interactions, and keeping the interface clean and distraction-free.
The goal wasn't simply to create more features—it was to create a better product.

Every project reaches a point where things stop working as expected, and SecureVault was no exception. From debugging application logic to refining the user interface, each obstacle became an opportunity to strengthen my problem-solving skills.

Rather than viewing these moments as setbacks, I began seeing them as one of the most valuable parts of the development process. Every issue solved improved both the application and my confidence as a developer.

SecureVault taught me that building software is about much more than writing code. Planning, testing, design, organization, and continuous improvement all play equally important roles in creating a successful application.
Perhaps the biggest lesson was understanding that users rarely notice complex implementation—they notice whether an application feels simple, reliable, and enjoyable to use.
That realization has influenced every project I've built since.

Although SecureVault has reached important milestones, I still see it as a project with room to grow. As I continue learning modern development practices, security concepts, and user experience design, I plan to keep refining the application with new ideas and improvements.
For me, SecureVault isn't simply a completed project—it's a reminder of how much can be learned by building something from the ground up.

Every project leaves behind more than code. It leaves lessons, experience, and confidence that shape future work. SecureVault continues to represent one of the most meaningful steps in my journey as a developer, proving that the best way to learn is by building products that solve real problems.

/blog/learning-ai-by-building.title: Learning AI by Building - Majd
/blog/learning-ai-by-building.description: Computer Science student and software developer passionate about building modern web experiences, Android applications, AI-powered tools, and intuitive user interfaces.
h2: The Balance Between AI and People
p: Rudraksh

Artificial intelligence is transforming the way software is built and experienced. Instead of only learning the theory, I chose to explore AI through hands-on projects, experimenting with modern models, APIs, and automation to better understand how intelligent applications solve real-world problems.
7 min read

Artificial intelligence has become one of the most exciting areas of modern software development. From intelligent assistants to creative tools and workflow automation, AI is changing how people interact with technology every day.

Rather than simply reading about these advancements, I wanted to understand how they worked by building applications that integrated AI into real experiences. Every experiment became an opportunity to explore how machine learning models, APIs, and intelligent systems could solve practical problems.

Like many developers, my interest in AI started with curiosity. I wanted to understand how applications could generate text, answer questions, recognize patterns, and assist users in ways that once seemed impossible.

That curiosity quickly became motivation to learn beyond documentation by experimenting with APIs, building prototypes, and understanding how AI could become part of everyday software instead of remaining a standalone technology.
Reading documentation provides knowledge, but building projects creates understanding.

Working with AI introduced challenges that extended beyond implementation. Prompt design, user experience, response quality, and application performance all became important parts of the development process.
Every prototype helped me better understand how AI should support users rather than replace thoughtful design.
One lesson became clear throughout my experiments: great AI products are not built by AI alone.

Behind every successful intelligent application is careful planning, meaningful user interaction, and thoughtful design decisions. AI works best when it enhances human creativity instead of adding unnecessary complexity.
This perspective continues to influence the way I approach every AI-powered project.
Artificial intelligence evolves at an incredible pace. New models, tools, and frameworks appear almost every week, making continuous learning essential.
Instead of trying to learn everything at once, I focus on building small, meaningful projects that introduce new concepts while strengthening my understanding through practical experience.
Each project becomes another step toward developing more capable and responsible AI applications.
AI will continue to shape the future of software, and I want to grow alongside it.

My goal is to build applications that combine modern development with intelligent features while maintaining simplicity, accessibility, and real value for users. Whether through automation, assistants, or creative tools, I see AI as an opportunity to build experiences that are more helpful, efficient, and engaging.
Learning AI is not about following trends—it's about understanding how technology can improve people's lives.

Every project I build teaches me something new, and every experiment strengthens my ability to create products that combine thoughtful design with intelligent functionality. For me, AI is not the destination—it's another step in the journey of becoming a better developer.

/blog/why-build-personal-projects.title: Why Build Personal Projects? - Majd
/blog/why-build-personal-projects.description: Computer Science student and software developer passionate about building modern web experiences, Android applications, AI-powered tools, and intuitive user interfaces.
h2: Every Project Starts With a Problem
Design Matters Just as Much as Code
p: Rudraksh

Every project I build is more than a technical exercise—it's an opportunity to solve real problems, explore new technologies, and grow as a developer. This article shares why personal projects have become the most valuable part of my learning journey.
7 min read

Personal projects are more than a way to practice programming—they're where ideas become reality. Every application I build represents an opportunity to explore new technologies, solve meaningful problems, and improve as a developer through hands-on experience. While courses and documentation provide the foundation, real growth happens when you start building something from scratch.

Each project teaches lessons that no tutorial can fully explain. From planning and design to debugging and deployment, every stage introduces new challenges that strengthen both technical and creative thinking. Over time, these experiences become far more valuable than simply completing exercises or following predefined steps.
Before writing a single line of code, I spend time understanding what I want to build and why it matters. Every project begins with questions such as:
What real problem does this solve?
Who will benefit from using it?
Which technologies best fit the idea?
How can I create an intuitive experience?
Answering these questions early creates a stronger foundation and helps every design and development decision remain focused on the user's needs.
No project follows a perfect path. Ideas evolve, designs change, and unexpected challenges appear along the way. Instead of avoiding mistakes, I treat them as part of the learning process.

Whether it's exploring a new framework, improving performance, or redesigning an interface, every iteration helps me understand software development more deeply. Experimentation has become one of the most rewarding parts of building products because every obstacle introduces an opportunity to improve.
Writing functional code is only part of creating great software. The way people experience a product often determines whether they continue using it.

I enjoy designing interfaces that feel simple, intuitive, and purposeful. Typography, spacing, interaction, and visual hierarchy all contribute to creating experiences that reduce complexity rather than add to it. Good design allows technology to feel effortless.

As a computer science student, I've realized that some of the most valuable lessons happen outside formal education. Personal projects encourage me to research unfamiliar concepts, explore modern development practices, and apply knowledge in practical situations.
Every project becomes a personal challenge to improve problem-solving, strengthen technical skills, and gain confidence through building rather than simply studying.

Technology evolves constantly, and so does my journey as a developer. I want to continue building products that solve real problems while exploring areas such as artificial intelligence, Android development, cloud technologies, and modern web experiences.
Every completed project is another step forward—not toward perfection, but toward becoming a better developer, designer, and lifelong learner.

The projects featured throughout this portfolio represent more than finished applications—they document my progress, curiosity, and commitment to continuous learning. Every build has taught me something valuable, and every future project will continue that journey.

/work/autofix-sh.title: AutoFix.sh - Majd
/work/autofix-sh.description: AutoFix.sh is an autonomous self-healing deployment platform that monitors GitHub Actions failures, diagnoses build and test errors, generates AI-powered code repairs, validates fixes inside isolated Docker sandboxes, and automatically opens pull requests with verified patches. It pairs a FastAPI repair engine with a real-time TanStack Start DevOps dashboard.
h2: From Broken Build to Verified PR
p: Rudraksh
Category
AI • DevOps
Year
Live Link

AutoFix.sh is an autonomous self-healing deployment platform that monitors GitHub Actions failures, diagnoses build and test errors, generates AI-powered code repairs, validates fixes inside isolated Docker sandboxes, and automatically opens pull requests with verified patches. It pairs a FastAPI repair engine with a real-time TanStack Start DevOps dashboard.

AutoFix.sh is an autonomous, AI-powered self-healing CI/CD deployment agent. It watches GitHub Actions failures, diagnoses what broke, generates code repairs, validates the fix in an isolated sandbox, and opens a pull request when the patch is safe.

The project is designed for modern DevOps teams that want deployment failures to become actionable repair workflows instead of manual debugging sessions. It turns failed logs into structured diagnosis, patch generation, verification, and review.

The repair flow starts when a GitHub Action fails. A FastAPI webhook handler fetches the raw logs, the parser extracts stack traces, failing files, and line numbers, then an AI classification agent uses GPT-4o to generate a high-confidence unified diff patch.

Before anything is committed, AutoFix.sh spins up a Docker sandbox, clones the repository at the failed commit, applies the patch, and runs the test suite. Only verified repairs move forward to a branch and automated GitHub pull request.
Real-time DevOps console

The frontend is a real-time DevOps console built with TanStack Start, React 19, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, Radix UI, Lucide icons, Vite, and Bun. Server-Sent Events stream repair timelines, command logs, status changes, and code diffs as the agent works.

The backend uses FastAPI, Uvicorn, Docker SDK, OpenAI’s Python API, PyGithub, Structlog, and an asynchronous in-memory event bus. Feature gates control confidence thresholds, sandbox execution, and dry-run commit behavior so automation stays observable and controlled.

CI failures often slow teams down because the error, diagnosis, fix, and verification are scattered across logs, local environments, and code review. AutoFix.sh compresses that loop into a repeatable repair pipeline.

The project shows how AI can be applied responsibly in developer tooling: not by blindly changing code, but by proposing fixes, validating them in isolation, and presenting verified patches for review.

/work/skillgap-ai.title: SkillGap AI - Majd
/work/skillgap-ai.description: SkillGap AI is an Innovex Hackathon project that analyzes a user’s current skills against a target role and generates a personalized roadmap, missing skills list, match score, and portfolio project ideas using Google Gemini AI. Built with React, TypeScript, Vite, Firebase, Tailwind CSS, Framer Motion, Radix UI, and shadcn/ui.
h2: AI Skill Analysis,Built for Career Growth
p: Rudraksh
Category
AI • Full Stack
Year
Live Link

SkillGap AI is an Innovex Hackathon project that analyzes a user’s current skills against a target role and generates a personalized roadmap, missing skills list, match score, and portfolio project ideas using Google Gemini AI. Built with React, TypeScript, Vite, Firebase, Tailwind CSS, Framer Motion, Radix UI, and shadcn/ui.

SkillGap AI is an AI-powered skill gap analyzer built for the Innovex Hackathon. Users enter their current skills and target role, then receive a personalized report that explains where they stand, what they are missing, and what to learn next.

The project uses Google Gemini AI to turn a simple profile input into practical career guidance: match score, missing skills, a structured learning roadmap, and project ideas tailored to the user’s target role.

The core flow is designed to be direct and useful. A user adds their skills, chooses a target role, and receives an AI-generated breakdown that feels actionable instead of generic. The result helps learners identify gaps and move from uncertainty to a clearer next step.

SkillGap AI generates a real skill gap report using Gemini 1.5 Flash, including a match score that shows how closely the user’s current skill set aligns with the selected role. The roadmap is ordered from foundational topics to more advanced learning steps.
The platform also suggests portfolio project ideas so users can turn learning into visible proof of ability, not just a list of topics to study.

The app is built with React 18, TypeScript, and Vite on the front end, with Tailwind CSS and Framer Motion powering the liquid glass interface and responsive interactions. Radix UI and shadcn/ui provide accessible interface components.

Firebase Auth supports email/password and Google sign-in, while Firestore stores user profiles, including full name, target role, and LinkedIn URL. React Router v6 handles routing across the analyzer, results, dashboard, login, register, profile, and settings pages.

SkillGap AI was created as a hackathon project with a practical goal: help learners understand what separates their current skills from the role they want. It combines AI output, authentication, user profiles, and a polished dashboard into a focused product experience.
Many learners know the role they want, but not the path to get there. SkillGap AI makes that path clearer by translating current skills into a concrete learning plan and portfolio direction.

The project turns AI into a practical navigator: assess the gap, understand priorities, and build projects that prove progress. It reflects a product mindset focused on solving a real learning problem with modern full-stack tools.

/work/securevault-v2.title: SecureVault V2.0 - Majd
/work/securevault-v2.description: SecureVault V2.0 is a secure file storage app built around drag-and-drop uploads, protected file handling, and useful metadata so users can organize private documents with confidence. Built with React, TypeScript, and Firebase, it focuses on a clean interface, fast access, and practical security-first workflows.
p: Rudraksh
Category
Full Stack • Cryptography
Year
Live Link

SecureVault V2.0 is a secure file storage app built around drag-and-drop uploads, protected file handling, and useful metadata so users can organize private documents with confidence. Built with React, TypeScript, and Firebase, it focuses on a clean interface, fast access, and practical security-first workflows.

SecureVault V2.0 is a secure file storage experience designed to make private file management feel simple, fast, and trustworthy. The project brings together drag-and-drop uploading, clean metadata handling, and a focused dashboard so files can be stored, reviewed, and organized without friction.
The goal was to move beyond a basic locker and create a more polished full-stack vault: one that feels modern in the browser while still keeping the security use case front and center.

The interface is built around a straightforward file flow. Users can add files quickly, attach meaningful metadata, and manage stored documents from a clear, responsive UI. React and TypeScript support the front-end structure, while Firebase provides the backend foundation for authentication, storage, and data handling.

SecureVault V2.0 uses React for the interface, TypeScript for safer application structure, and Firebase for cloud-backed storage workflows. The stack keeps the project lightweight while still supporting real product behavior: uploads, metadata, authenticated access, and scalable data organization.
The technical focus was to keep the product usable first: every security feature had to feel approachable rather than hidden behind a complicated interface.

Metadata is a key part of the project. Instead of treating files as anonymous uploads, SecureVault V2.0 gives each stored item context, making it easier to identify, organize, and retrieve important documents later.
This makes the app feel closer to a real document vault than a simple upload demo.

The project demonstrates full-stack thinking: front-end usability, backend storage, structured data, and a security-focused product concept working together. It shows how a focused tool can be small in scope but still feel complete and practical.
SecureVault V2.0 reflects a builder mindset: taking a real-world need—private file storage—and turning it into a usable web app with a clean interface and reliable architecture.

The project balances security, usability, and speed. It is designed for people who want a simple way to store sensitive files, understand what each file contains, and access their vault without unnecessary complexity.

/work/find-my-subsidy.title: FindMySubsidy - Majd
/work/find-my-subsidy.description: FindMySubsidy is a premium map-first platform that helps founders, MSMEs, manufacturers, and industry teams discover government subsidies, schemes, grants, incentives, and support programs across Indian states. It replaces static PDFs and outdated portals with a responsive discovery workspace built around an interactive India map, searchable scheme lists, and clear application guidance.
p: Rudraksh
Category
GovTech • Full Stack
Year
Live Link

FindMySubsidy is a premium map-first platform that helps founders, MSMEs, manufacturers, and industry teams discover government subsidies, schemes, grants, incentives, and support programs across Indian states. It replaces static PDFs and outdated portals with a responsive discovery workspace built around an interactive India map, searchable scheme lists, and clear application guidance.

FindMySubsidy is a premium, interactive platform that helps users discover government subsidies, schemes, grants, incentives, and support programs available across Indian states. It was designed for founders, MSMEs, manufacturers, and industry teams who need a faster way to navigate government policy.
Instead of sending users through static PDFs and scattered portals, the project creates a visual discovery workspace where the map, state details, and schemes all work together in one clear flow.

The core experience starts with an interactive India map. Users can explore states visually, hover to see active scheme counts, zoom and pan around smaller states, and select a state to reveal relevant programs. This makes the “click state → discover subsidies” workflow immediately understandable.

The platform pairs the map with a dynamic state schemes list, allowing users to browse incentives by sector and search by scheme name, department, or keyword. Each program can surface key benefits, eligibility criteria, required documents, and official portal links in structured visual panels.
Application timeline steppers help explain the process from registration to verification and disbursement, turning complex policy information into a more guided experience.

FindMySubsidy is built with TanStack Start, React 19, and TanStack Router for fast server-side rendering and routing. Tailwind CSS and Lightning CSS handle the styling system, while react-simple-maps and TopoJSON power the scalable India map.
Framer Motion adds smooth drawer slide-overs and list entry animations, Lucide icons support the interface, and Vite with Nitro prepares the app for serverless deployment on Vercel.

The project focuses on making dense government information easier to scan and act on. By combining a visual map, searchable data, and structured benefit panels, it gives users a modern way to understand what support may be available in their state.
The result is a practical GovTech-style product that turns policy discovery into a guided, responsive, and more approachable workflow.

Government schemes can be valuable, but they are often difficult to find, compare, and understand. FindMySubsidy improves that discovery layer by giving users one place to explore state-level support visually and contextually.

The project shows how thoughtful interface design and modern web tooling can make public information easier to access. It is built as a focused tool for founders and teams who need clarity before they can take action.
